content.text: Before writing a single line of React, you need two things on your machine:

. Node.js provides the JavaScript runtime that powers every build tool you will use (Vite, TypeScript, ESLint, etc.). The package manager fetches, installs, and tracks the open-source libraries your project depends on. Neither is optional — they are the foundation of the modern React workflow.
Node.js is a JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 engine. It lets you run JavaScript
the browser — on your laptop, a server, or a CI machine.
A common point of confusion: React itself runs
. Node.js does not ship to your users. It is purely a
— it runs the bundler that compiles your code, the dev server you open in your browser, and the test runner that checks your logic. Once you
, the output is plain HTML, CSS, and browser JavaScript. No Node.js required to serve it.

# Check that Node is installed:
node --version   # e.g. v20.11.0 (LTS)
npm --version    # e.g. 10.2.4

# Run any JS file locally:
node script.js
for React projects in 2024. Odd-numbered releases (17, 19, 21) are short-lived; even-numbered LTS releases (18, 20) are supported for three years.
install Node from the official site directly if you plan to work on multiple projects — different projects often need different Node versions. Use a version manager instead:

# After installing nvm:
nvm install 20       # install Node 20 LTS
nvm use 20           # activate it in this shell
nvm alias default 20 # make it the default for new shells

# Pin the version for a project (nvm reads .nvmrc):
echo '20' > .nvmrc
nvm use              # reads .nvmrc automatically
A package manager does three things: it
which versions of each library are compatible,
them from the npm registry, and
the exact versions in a lock file so every developer and CI machine gets identical installs.

attrs.alt: Comparison table of npm, yarn, and pnpm commands, lock files, and storage models
attrs.title: npm vs yarn vs pnpm

content.text: comes bundled with Node.js — install Node and you have npm. It is the oldest of the three and the most widely documented. The npm registry (
) is where all three package managers download packages from by default.

npm install               # install from package.json
npm install react         # add as dependency
npm install -D vitest     # add as devDependency
npm uninstall lodash      # remove a package
npm run dev               # run the 'dev' script
npm run build             # run the 'build' script
npm outdated              # show outdated packages
npm update                # update packages within semver range

Yarn was created by Meta in 2016 to solve early npm performance and reliability problems. Modern npm has closed most of that gap, but Yarn (v1/Classic) remains common in older codebases. Yarn Berry (v2+) introduced Plug'n'Play (PnP) which eliminates
entirely — it is a significant architectural change that is not universally compatible.

# Install yarn via corepack (Node 16.10+):
corepack enable
corepack prepare yarn@stable --activate

yarn install      # install from package.json
yarn add react    # add dependency
yarn add -D vitest # add devDependency
yarn remove lodash
yarn dev          # run scripts without 'run' keyword
yarn build
— packages are stored once on your hard drive and linked (not copied) into each project's
. This makes installs significantly faster on subsequent projects and saves gigabytes of disk space. pnpm is the package manager used by Vite's own monorepo.

# Install pnpm via corepack:
corepack enable
corepack prepare pnpm@latest --activate

pnpm install      # install from package.json
pnpm add react    # add dependency
pnpm add -D vitest
pnpm remove lodash
pnpm dev          # run scripts
pnpm build
Every Node.js / React project has a
at its root. This is the single source of truth for your project's identity, dependencies, and runnable scripts.
— libraries needed at runtime in the browser (React, React Router, etc.).
— tools used only during development and build (Vite, TypeScript, ESLint). Not bundled into the final output.
— prevents accidental publish to the npm registry.
, the package manager resolves the full dependency tree (including transitive dependencies — dependencies of your dependencies) and writes the exact resolved versions to a lock file:
Always commit the lock file to git.
on a teammate's machine or in CI installs exactly the same versions you tested with — down to the patch number. Without it, a dependency could silently upgrade between installs and introduce a bug.
When a package manager installs dependencies, they land in a
folder in your project root. This folder can be hundreds of megabytes for a typical React project. You
commit it — always add it to
Anyone who clones the repo runs
from the lock file. The lock file is small and deterministic;
is large and reproducible from it.
. The prefix symbol controls how loosely the package manager is allowed to upgrade:
The lock file pins the actual resolved version regardless of the range in
. The range only matters when you run

# With npm:
npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react-ts
cd my-app
npm install
npm run dev

# With yarn:
yarn create vite my-app --template react-ts
cd my-app
yarn
yarn dev

# With pnpm:
pnpm create vite my-app --template react-ts
cd my-app
pnpm install
pnpm dev

git clone https://github.com/org/repo.git
cd repo

# Use the same package manager as the project
# (check which lock file exists):
ls package-lock.json   # → npm install
ls yarn.lock           # → yarn install
ls pnpm-lock.yaml      # → pnpm install

npm audit             # list known security issues
npm audit fix         # auto-fix non-breaking issues
npm audit fix --force # force-fix (may include breaking changes — review carefully)
Choosing a Package Manager for a New Project
For a new React project, all three managers work equally well with the npm registry. The practical advice:
— zero setup (comes with Node). Good for solo projects and beginners.
— choose this only if you are joining a team already using it. Starting new projects with Classic is not recommended.
— recommended for monorepos or machines with many projects (saves disk space and installs faster). Vite's own tooling uses it.
Never mix package managers in a single project. If a repo has a
in a yarn project generates a conflicting
and can cause subtle version mismatches.
npx — Running Package Binaries Without Installing
(bundled with npm 5.2+) lets you run a CLI tool from the npm registry without installing it globally. It downloads the package on demand, runs it, then discards it:

# Create a Vite project without globally installing create-vite:
npx create-vite my-app

# Run a specific version of a tool:
npx prettier@3 --write src/**/*.ts

# Run a locally installed tool (in node_modules/.bin):
npx tsc --version   # uses the local TypeScript, not a global one
This is why the Vite quick-start command is
is a sign two people used different managers. Delete the wrong one and
, then reinstall with the correct tool.
installs into your Node version, not the project. When the project's team mates run
, they do not get it. Prefer
Skipping the lock file in CI
) in CI pipelines. It installs exactly what is in the lock file and errors if the lock file is out of sync with
— never do this. It bloats the repo with hundreds of thousands of files and causes constant merge conflicts.
— Vite 5 requires Node ≥18. Using Node 16 causes cryptic errors. Check
if you see unexpected install failures.
is where you define runnable commands for the project. Scripts run in a shell that has
prepended to the PATH, so you can use locally-installed CLI tools directly by name.

{
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "vite",
    "build": "tsc && vite build",
    "preview": "vite preview",
    "test": "vitest",
    "test:ui": "vitest --ui",
    "test:coverage": "vitest run --coverage",
    "lint": "eslint src --ext .ts,.tsx",
    "lint:fix": "eslint src --ext .ts,.tsx --fix",
    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit",
    "format": "prettier --write src"
  }
}
Scripts can compose each other using the package manager's run command:
) run automatically before or after their base script. For example, a
— commonly used to patch packages or run code-generation steps.
— a single repository containing multiple packages that can reference each other. This is the foundation of monorepo tooling (Turborepo, Nx, etc.).
at the root installs all dependencies for every workspace into a single
(hoisted). Packages in the same workspace can import each other by name without publishing to npm.
Keeping dependencies up to date is an ongoing maintenance task. The key commands:

# See what can be updated:
npm outdated

# Update all packages within their semver range:
npm update

# Update a specific package (may break semver range):
npm install react@latest

# Interactive upgrade tool (install separately):
npx npm-check-updates -u   # updates package.json versions
npm install                # installs the updated versions

# pnpm equivalent:
pnpm update --latest       # upgrade all to latest, ignoring semver
checks the npm advisory database for known vulnerabilities in your dependency tree. Many CI pipelines run this as a required gate:

npm audit               # prints vulnerability report
npm audit --json        # machine-readable output for CI
npm audit fix           # auto-fix compatible issues

# Fail CI if any high/critical vulnerabilities exist:
npm audit --audit-level=high
Per-project configuration for the package manager goes in

# .npmrc

# Use a private registry for scoped packages:
@myorg:registry=https://npm.pkg.github.com

# Set engine-strict to fail on wrong Node version:
engine-strict=true

# Cache location (pnpm-specific):
store-dir=~/.pnpm-store
declares the required Node version. Combined with
, this prevents install on incompatible Node versions:
(shipped with Node 16.10+) manages which version of yarn or pnpm a project uses. You declare the version in
and corepack ensures everyone on the team uses exactly that version:

corepack enable           # enable corepack shims
corepack use pnpm@9.1.0  # sets "packageManager" and downloads it
pnpm install              # uses exactly pnpm 9.1.0
This solves the "works on my machine" problem for the build toolchain itself, not just the npm packages.
A typical CI pipeline for a React project uses the package manager in three distinct ways: installing, caching, and running scripts. Here is a GitHub Actions example:

# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'   # caches ~/.npm between runs

      - run: npm ci           # exact lock-file install (not npm install)
      - run: npm run typecheck
      - run: npm run lint
      - run: npm run test
      - run: npm run build
Key differences between CI and local development:
first, then installs exactly the lock file. It errors if
in CI — it may silently update packages if the lock file diverges, making builds non-reproducible.
When a package installs unexpectedly, or two packages require incompatible versions of the same dependency, inspection tools help diagnose the issue:

# Show full dependency tree:
npm ls

# Find who depends on a specific package:
npm ls react

# Find why a package is installed (pnpm):
pnpm why react

# Deduplicate packages (npm):
npm dedupe

# Show the size of each installed package:
npx cost-of-modules  # third-party tool
npx bundlephobia-cli react  # check bundle size before installing

Peer dependency warnings (e.g., "react-dom requires react@^18") appear during install when a library expects a certain version of another library that is not installed. You usually need to install the peer dependency yourself — it is not installed automatically in npm v7+.
Node.js is a JavaScript runtime that powers React build tooling. Your browser apps do not ship Node.js — it is a development-only dependency.
Use a version manager (nvm, fnm) so you can switch Node versions per project and pin the version in
all install from the same npm registry. Choose one per project and be consistent.
defines dependencies and scripts; the lock file pins exact versions;
holds the installed code. Commit only the first two.
runs a package binary on demand without a global install — essential for scaffolding tools like
in CI pipelines to guarantee a reproducible, lock-file-exact install.
